food_subgroup: Deep-fried rice flour tart shell filled with braised jicama and carrot, topped with prawn and omelette shreds

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Kueh Pie Tee. No sufficiently specific FoodOn class covers this Peranakan deep-fried rice flour tart shell with braised jicama filling. Closest parent would be a generic fried pastry snack class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fried rice flour tart (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GBQ used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 entry exists for Kueh Pie Tee.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Kueh Pie Tee.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 1 piece (~30g), providing approximately 65 kcal; a typical appetiser serving at a hawker centre or Peranakan restaurant is 4–5 pieces (~120–150g).

nutrient_source.title: Kueh Pie Tee — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore food composition database entry for Kueh Pie Tee. All per-100g values are taken from this verified record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Flour-Based Fried Snacks — analogous estimate based on deep-fried rice flour products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kueh Pie Tee in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~55 is estimated from the closest analogous deep-fried rice flour snack entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The deep-fried rice flour shell is the primary carbohydrate source; jicama filling has negligible GI impact. GL per piece = (55 × 8g available carbohydrate per 30g piece) / 100 ≈ 4.4. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — 367mg per 100g (~110mg per piece); escalates with serving size and condiment use
hypertension.guidance: A single piece contributes ~110mg sodium, which is low, but a 4–5 piece serving delivers ~440–550mg sodium; patients with hypertension should limit to 2–3 pieces and avoid additional chilli or soy-based dipping sauces to keep sodium within a single-meal budget of 600–800mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~55) and very low glycemic load per piece (GL ~4.4); jicama filling provides dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 and a GL of ~4.4 per piece, Kueh Pie Tee is one of the lower-GL hawker snacks available; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume 3–4 pieces as a snack or appetiser without significant postprandial glucose excursion, provided total carbohydrate intake for the meal is managed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.7g/100g, ~0.5g per piece) and zero trans fat; moderate total fat from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The thin-walled shell geometry limits oil absorption during deep-frying, resulting in low saturated fat (~0.5g per piece) and confirmed zero trans fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease can include 3–4 pieces as an occasional snack, monitoring total fat intake across the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat; presence of Vitamin C and zinc from prawn and jicama
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Low saturated fat content and the absence of coconut milk make this a relatively low-inflammatory snack; the jicama provides small amounts of Vitamin C (~1.5mg per piece) and prawn contributes zinc (~0.15mg per piece) and selenium (~2.4mcg per piece), which may modestly support antioxidant status.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kueh Pie Tee (顶帽饼, also called 'top hat pastry') is a classic Peranakan / Nyonya appetiser from Singapore. It consists of a thin, crispy deep-fried rice flour tart shell — shaped like a miniature top hat using a brass mould — filled with braised jicama (bangkuang) and carrot, then topped with a whole prawn, omelette shreds, and chilli sauce. Each piece weighs approximately 30g and provides ~65 kcal, 2g protein, 3g fat, and 8g carbohydrate. The shell is made by dipping a heated brass mould into a thin rice flour batter and submerging it in hot oil until the cup releases and crisps. The filling is a slow-braised mixture of shredded jicama, carrot, dried shrimp, and soy sauce. Nutritionally, Kueh Pie Tee is one of the lowest-calorie and lowest-glycemic-load hawker kueh options available in Singapore, with a GI of approximately 55 and a GL of only ~4.4 per piece. Sodium is moderate at ~367mg per 100g (~110mg per piece). Saturated fat is low at 1.7g per 100g. The food is gluten-free and dairy-free. It is available at Peranakan restaurants, hawker centres, and specialty kueh shops such as Bengawan Solo. Source: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are present and verified. GI is estimated from the closest analogous deep-fried rice flour snack in the University of Sydney GI Database with documented basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kueh Pie Tee not available in the HPB Singapore database as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Singapore database entry for this food. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB Singapore source. GI ~55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on deep-fried rice flour snack entries; no direct GI measurement for Kueh Pie Tee exists in the database. Halal status is not confirmed — traditional Peranakan recipes use dried shrimp and prawn which are permissible, but preparation environment halal certification varies by vendor; is_halal is set to false pending vendor-level certification. The food_subgroup in the input contained truncated text; this has been normalised to a clean descriptive string.
